title: More Prefixes: re-, over-, under-, out-

explanation.rule: These prefixes change the meaning of verbs and adjectives to express doing something again, excessively, insufficiently, or better.

examples.definition: to arrange again in a different way
to react more strongly than necessary
to describe as greater than it really is
to describe as less important than it is
to do better than someone else

wordForms.example: The committee decided to reassess the funding application.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to evaluate or measure the value of something

wordForms.definition: to change the arrangement of something
wordForms.example: She rearranged the furniture to make more space.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to put things in order; to plan

wordForms.definition: to express something in a different way
wordForms.example: Could you rephrase your question more clearly?
After years apart, they reconnected at a school reunion.

wordForms.definition: to respond more strongly than necessary
wordForms.example: Try not to overreact to every small setback.
Don't overdo the exercise if your back is still sore.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to carry out an action or task

wordForms.definition: to exaggerate the importance of something
wordForms.example: It would be hard to overstate the importance of this decision.

wordForms.definition: to pay someone less than they deserve
wordForms.example: Many workers in the sector are significantly underpaid.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to give money in exchange for work

wordForms.definition: to represent something as less than it really is
wordForms.example: The report tended to understate the scale of the problem.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to say or express in words

wordForms.definition: to do better than someone else
wordForms.example: Each year, the teams try to outdo each other with their costumes.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to carry out an action or task

wordForms.example: She received an award for her outstanding contribution to research.

wordForms.definition: to place too high a value on something
wordForms.example: Analysts warned that investors were overvaluing the shares.
wordForms.rootDefinition: the importance or worth assigned to something

multipleChoiceExercises.sentence: We need to ___ the plan before the presentation.
Don't ___ the spices – a little goes a long way.
Many low-income workers are severely ___ for the work they do.
She tends to ___ when things go wrong, even slightly.
The team's ___ performance earned them three international awards.
Could you ___ that sentence? I didn't quite follow it.

transformExercises.sentence: The company ___ its prices after realising the market had changed. (ASSESS)
Please ___ the chairs before the guests arrive. (ARRANGE)
It would be impossible to ___ the damage caused by the storm. (STATE)
Workers in that industry are routinely ___. (PAY)
Each candidate tried to ___ the others with their ideas. (DO)
After losing signal, the phone failed to ___ to the network. (CONNECT)
